## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BM
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
6	`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
7	`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
8	`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
9	across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
10	fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
11	enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
12	work, prone to silent drift.
13	
14	There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
15	`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
16	uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
17	the sweep.
18	
19	The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
20	messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
21	durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
22	discovery recipe, not here.
23	
24	## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay
25	
26	"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
27	literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
28	character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
29	API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
30	textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
31	`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
32	build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.
33	
34	MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
35	an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
36	the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
37	prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
38	(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
39	inventing rationale.
40	
41	Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
42	dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
43	local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
44	(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
45	one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
46	text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
47	concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.
48	
49	## Convention — locked
50	
51	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:
52	
53	- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
54	- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
55	             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
56	             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
57	- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
58	             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
59	             `e` = environment, etc.)
60	- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
61	- `_<value>` — the canonical value name
62	
63	Full target table (all in-scope enums):
64	
65	| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
66	|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
67	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
68	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
69	| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
70	| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
71	| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
72	| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |
73	
74	Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
75	egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
76	share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
77	DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
78	variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
79	whole cluster.
80	
81	## Scope decisions — resolved
82	
83	- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
84	- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
85	  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
86	  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
87	- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
88	  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
89	  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
90	- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
91	  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
92	  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
93	  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
94	  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
95	  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
96	  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
97	  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
98	- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
99	  unchanged from original framing).
100	
101	## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work
102	
103	Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
104	the candidate list and classify each hit:
105	
106	1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
107	   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
108	2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
109	   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
110	   **Migrate.**
111	3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
112	   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
113	   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
114	   alone.**
115	4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
116	   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
117	   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
118	   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
119	   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
120	     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
121	   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
122	   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
123	   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
124	   `-tether`.
125	
126	### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses
127	
128	- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
129	  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
130	  `:-bind`, etc.
131	- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
132	  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
133	  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
134	  stale.
135	- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
136	  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
137	  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
138	  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
139	  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).
140	
141	### Noise ranking (classification effort)
142	
143	Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
144	prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
145	`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
146	class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
147	is the heavy classification grind.
148	
149	## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate
150	
151	Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
152	(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
153	context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
154	literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
155	declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
156	remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
157	until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.
158	
159	**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
160	sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
161	graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
162	suites.
163	
164	## Discovery recipe
165	
166	- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
167	  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
168	  enum missed.
169	- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
170	  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
171	- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
172	  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
173	  tables.
174	- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
175	  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
176	  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.
177	
178	## Posture
179	
180	**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
181	execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
182	pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.
183	
184	**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
185	`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
186	gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
187	remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
188	above.
189	
190	## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)
191	
192	₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
193	(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
194	not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
195	concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
196	wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
197	is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
198	intact.
199	
200	What remains and matters:
201	- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
202	  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
203	  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
204	  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
205	- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
206	  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
207	- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
208	  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
209	  any late bugfix churn there.
210	
211	## What done looks like
212	
213	- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
214	  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
215	- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
216	  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
217	  profiles) updated in place.
218	- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
219	  their selecting dispatch migrated.
220	- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
221	- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
222	- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
223	  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
224	  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
225	
226	## Out of scope
227	
228	- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
229	  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
230	- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
231	  by decision above.
232	- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
233	- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.
234	
235	## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)
236	
237	Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:
238	
239	1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
240	   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
241	   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
242	   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
243	2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
244	   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
245	3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
246	   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
247	   graft cloud build.
248	4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
249	   heavy classification.
250	5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
251	6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.
252	
253	Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
254	not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.
255	
256	# jjezs_pace ₢BMAAA
257	
258	## Character
259	
260	Codification. The synthesis already lives in the paddock; this pace gives it
261	a permanent home a future minter will find. Light judgment on *where* the
262	section lives.
263	
264	## Goal
265	
266	The sprue convention exists as a written spec section: grounded in
267	`mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay`, with the full target table, the four-class
268	use-site model (incl. class-4 "coupled derived string stays bare", two
269	witnesses), and the `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
270	
271	## Boundary
272	
273	Documentation only — no value migration.
274	
275	## Recipe
276	
277	- Cite `mcm_sprue` (the values are wire-level literal tokens) and
278	  `mcm_inlay` (prefix discipline makes each sprue prefix-recognized). Do
279	  not invent rationale the MCM quoins already carry.
280	- Pick the host doc at mount: a regime spec (RBRN/RBRV) section, or a
281	  convention section near the BUS0/RBS0 vocabulary. Decide by where a future
282	  minter would look first.
283	- Lift the convention + four-class model + exclusion rationale from the
284	  paddock; do not re-derive.
285	
286	## Acceptance
287	
288	- Spec renders cleanly; convention + class-4 rule + RBRN_RUNTIME exclusion
289	  are discoverable without reading this heat's history, and grounded in the
290	  existing MCM vocabulary.

# jjezs_paddock ₣BM

## Context

Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
work, prone to silent drift.

There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
the sweep.

The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
discovery recipe, not here.

## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay

"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.

MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
inventing rationale.

Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Full target table (all in-scope enums):

| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |

Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
whole cluster.

## Scope decisions — resolved

- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
  unchanged from original framing).

## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
the candidate list and classify each hit:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
   **Migrate.**
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
   alone.**
4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
   `-tether`.

### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses

- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
  `:-bind`, etc.
- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
  stale.
- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).

### Noise ranking (classification effort)

Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
is the heavy classification grind.

## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate

Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.

**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
suites.

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
  enum missed.
- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
  tables.
- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
above.

## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)

₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
intact.

What remains and matters:
- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
  any late bugfix churn there.

## What done looks like

- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
  profiles) updated in place.
- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
  their selecting dispatch migrated.
- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.

## Out of scope

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
  by decision above.
- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:

1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
   graft cloud build.
4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
   heavy classification.
5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.

Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

a permanent home a future minter will find. Light judgment on *where* the
section lives.

## Goal

`mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay`, with the full target table, the four-class
use-site model (incl. class-4 "coupled derived string stays bare", two

Mounted ₣BM. Here's the orientation.

## Next pace: ₢BMAAA (sprue-convention-spec-authoring) [rough]

**Goal in one sentence:** Give the sprue-naming convention a permanent written home in a spec — grounded in `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay`, carrying the full target table, the four-class use-site model (including the class-4 "coupled derived string stays bare" rule with its two witnesses), and the `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale — so a future minter finds it without reading this heat's history.

**Heat context:** `rbk-12-mvp-regime-sprues` (₣BM) — migrating bare regime enum values (`conjure`, `tether`, `disabled`…) to prefix-discoverable sprues (`rbnve_conjure`, `rbnve_tether`, `rbnne_disabled`…). The egress-mode pilot (₢BMAAB) already landed and proved the mechanical pattern + class-4 distinction; fast suite green. This pace codifies the convention before the heavier vessel/node/bubep migrations.

**Boundary:** Documentation only — no value migration in this pace.

**Name assessment:** Silks fit the docket cleanly. No rename.

## Approach

The one real judgment call the docket leaves open is *where the section lives*. The convention is cross-cutting — it governs RBK regimes (RBRV/RBRN) **and** the BUK regime (`BURN_PLATFORM` → `bunne_*`), and it grounds in MCM vocabulary. So a regime-specific spec (RBRN or RBRV) under-houses it. My instinct:

- **Candidate A:** A convention section in a regime/vocabulary spec near where regime enums are *declared* (the `buv_enum_enroll` / BUS0 territory) — since the convention is fundamentally about how enum *values* are minted, and BUS0 owns the enum-enroll vocabulary that spans both projects.
- **Candidate B:** RBS0/RBSRV regime spec section — discoverable to an RBK minter but weaker for the BUK-side `bunne_*` reach.

I'd lean A but want to look at the actual spec structure (BUS0 enum-enroll vocabulary, RBS0 top) before committing. Then I lift the convention + four-class model + exclusion rationale verbatim-in-substance from the paddock, citing the MCM quoins rather than re-deriving rationale.

**Execution strategy:** Sequential, single doc, opus (placement judgment + MCM-grounding fidelity matter more than mechanical edits here).

= Vox Liturgical Specification
:doctype: book

// tag::mapping-section[]
//
// Category declarations:
// vosl:    Liturgy (non-terminal, naming system umbrella)
// voslc_:  Liturgy Cipher (project root prefix)
// vosls_:  Liturgy Signet (complete prefix before separator)
// vosle_:  Liturgy Epithet (descriptive word after separator)
// vosli_:  Liturgy Inscription (full artifact name)
// voslv_:  Liturgy Vesture (domain construction rules)
// vosln_:  Liturgy Ensign (signet extension that classifies category)
// voslm_:  Liturgy Monogram (signet extension that binds to epithet initial)
// vosld:   Liturgy Domain (non-terminal, domain-specific vestures)
// vosldr_: Liturgy Domain Rust (Rust source vesture)
// vosldb_: Liturgy Domain Bash (Bash source vesture)
// voslda_: Liturgy Domain AsciiDoc (AsciiDoc attribute vesture)
// vosldp_: Liturgy Domain Publication (spec/publication vesture)
// vosldg_: Liturgy Domain Git (git ref vesture)
// voslds_: Liturgy Domain Slash (slash command vesture)
//
// Variant patterns:
//   _s: plural form
//   _p: possessive form
//

// Liturgy (naming system)
:voslc_cipher:          <<voslc_cipher,Cipher>>
:voslc_cipher_s:        <<voslc_cipher,Ciphers>>
:voslc_cipher_p:        <<voslc_cipher,Cipher's>>
:vosls_signet:          <<vosls_signet,Signet>>
:vosls_signet_s:        <<vosls_signet,Signets>>
:vosle_epithet:         <<vosle_epithet,Epithet>>
:vosle_epithet_s:       <<vosle_epithet,Epithets>>
:vosli_inscription:     <<vosli_inscription,Inscription>>
:vosli_inscription_s:   <<vosli_inscription,Inscriptions>>
:voslv_vesture:         <<voslv_vesture,Vesture>>
:voslv_vesture_s:       <<voslv_vesture,Vestures>>
:vosln_ensign:          <<vosln_ensign,Ensign>>
:vosln_ensign_s:        <<vosln_ensign,Ensigns>>
:voslm_monogram:        <<voslm_monogram,Monogram>>
:voslm_monogram_s:      <<voslm_monogram,Monograms>>

// Liturgy Domains (vesture specializations)
:vosldr_rust:           <<vosldr_rust,Rust Vesture>>
:vosldb_bash:           <<vosldb_bash,Bash Vesture>>
:voslda_asciidoc:       <<voslda_asciidoc,AsciiDoc Vesture>>
:vosldp_publication:    <<vosldp_publication,Publication Vesture>>
:vosldg_git:            <<vosldg_git,Git Vesture>>
:voslds_slash:          <<voslds_slash,Slash Command Vesture>>

// end::mapping-section[]

== Overview

The Vox Liturgical Specification defines the universal naming system for artifacts across all specifications.
A
{voslc_cipher}
seeds the namespace;
{vosls_signet_s}
build on it;
{vosle_epithet_s}
provide human-readable descriptions;
{vosli_inscription_s}
are complete names;
{voslv_vesture_s}
define domain-specific construction rules.

This vocabulary applies to ALL specifications — VOS (kit distribution), BUS (bash dispatch), JJSA (gallops data), and others.
The liturgy is the shared language for naming things.

For tabtarget dispatch vocabulary (Colophon, Frontispiece, Imprint, Formulary, Launcher), see BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc.

== Core Liturgy Terms

[[voslc_cipher]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{voslc_cipher}::
A 2-5 character project root that seeds the namespace.
The
{voslc_cipher}
is globally unique across all projects and serves as the foundation for all derived
{vosls_signet_s}.
Lowercase letters only; no
{voslc_cipher}
may be a prefix of another (terminal exclusivity).
+
Single source of truth: `Tools/vok/vof/src/vofc_registry.rs`
+
See <<cipher_registry>> for declaration pattern and the complete list.

[[vosls_signet]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{vosls_signet}::
The complete prefix before the separator in an
{vosli_inscription}.
A
{vosls_signet}
is derived from a
{voslc_cipher}
by extending with additional characters that respect terminal exclusivity.
The
{vosls_signet}
is the identity stamp that binds an artifact to its owning project.
+
Examples: `jjk` (kit), `jjc` (command prefix), `voslc` (this term's signet).
+
A
{vosls_signet}
is extended from its
{voslc_cipher}
using either an
{vosln_ensign}
(classification marker) or a
{voslm_monogram}
(epithet-initial binding).

[[vosln_ensign]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{vosln_ensign}::
A
{vosls_signet}
extension that classifies artifact category.
The terminal character(s) mark a type or role, independent of the
{vosle_epithet}.
+
Examples:
+
* `axt_string` — `t` = type classification, `string` = epithet
* `burc_regime` — `rc` = regime-config classification, `regime` = epithet
* `axtu_path` — `tu` = type-universal sub-classification, `path` = epithet
+
{vosln_ensign_s}
enable hierarchical categorization within a
{voslc_cipher}
namespace.

[[voslm_monogram]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{voslm_monogram}::
A
{vosls_signet}
extension where the terminal character binds to the
{vosle_epithet}
initial.
Creates a mnemonic connection between
{vosls_signet}
and
{vosle_epithet}.
+
Examples:
+
* `buc_command` — `c` = first letter of `command`
* `jjw_workbench` — `w` = first letter of `workbench`
* `voslc_cipher` — `c` = first letter of `cipher`
+
{voslm_monogram_s}
create tight signet-epithet binding but limit each terminal letter to one artifact.

[[vosle_epithet]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{vosle_epithet}::
The descriptive word after the separator in an
{vosli_inscription}.
Provides human-readable meaning while the
{vosls_signet}
provides machine-parseable identity.
+
Examples: `cipher` in `voslc_cipher`, `heat` in `jjc-heat-mount`.

[[vosli_inscription]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{vosli_inscription}::
The full artifact name combining
{vosls_signet},
separator, and
{vosle_epithet}.
An
{vosli_inscription}
is the complete, unambiguous identifier for any named artifact.
+
Structure: `{signet}{separator}{epithet}`
+
Examples: `voslc_cipher` (underscore separator), `jjc-heat-mount` (hyphen separator).
Domain
{voslv_vesture_s}
may specify additional completion rules (see individual domain definitions).

[[voslv_vesture]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{voslv_vesture}::
Domain-specific construction rules for
{vosli_inscription_s}.
Each
{voslv_vesture}
defines signet_case, separator, epithet_case, and envelope for its domain.
Different domains (Rust, Bash, AsciiDoc, etc.) have different naming conventions.
+
A
{voslv_vesture}
is like clothing for a domain — it dresses the abstract naming concepts in domain-appropriate form.

== Liturgy Domains

[[vosldr_rust]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{vosldr_rust}::
{voslv_vesture}
for Rust source identifiers.
+
* signet_case: snake_case
* separator: `_`
* epithet_case: PascalCase for types, snake_case for functions
* envelope: none

[[vosldb_bash]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{vosldb_bash}::
{voslv_vesture}
for Bash source identifiers.
+
* signet_case: lowercase
* separator: `_`
* epithet_case: snake_case
* envelope: `.sh` for files

[[voslda_asciidoc]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{voslda_asciidoc}::
{voslv_vesture}
for AsciiDoc attribute identifiers.
+
* signet_case: lowercase
* separator: `_`
* epithet_case: snake_case
* envelope: none (attributes), `[[` `]]` for anchors

[[vosldp_publication]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{vosldp_publication}::
{voslv_vesture}
for publication and specification files.
+
* signet_case: UPPERCASE
* separator: `-`
* epithet_case: PascalCase
* envelope: `.adoc`, `.md`

[[vosldg_git]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{vosldg_git}::
{voslv_vesture}
for git refs.
+
* signet_case: lowercase
* separator: `/`
* epithet_case: kebab-case
* envelope: none

[[voslds_slash]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{voslds_slash}::
{voslv_vesture}
for slash command identifiers.
+
* signet_case: lowercase
* separator: `-`
* epithet_case: kebab-case
* envelope: `.md` for files

== Terminal Exclusivity

A fundamental constraint on
{voslc_cipher_s}
and
{vosls_signet_s}:
a prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both.

Examples:

* `rbg` has children (`rbga`, `rbgb`...) → `rbg` cannot name a thing
* `rbi` names Image module → `rbia`, `rbib` forbidden

This rule ensures unambiguous prefix matching for operations like uninstall (which removes all files matching a
{vosls_signet}).

Validated at runtime by `vofc_validate_terminal_exclusivity()` in `vofc_registry.rs`.

== Minting Workflow

Before minting new prefixes:

1. **Enumerate namespaces** — list every place this name will appear (code, refs, commands, env vars, target paths...)
2. **Check reserved suffixes** — ensure the suffix matches intended type within the kit
3. **Verify terminal exclusivity** — search existing trees, ensure no conflicts
4. **Document the allocation** — add to prefix map in relevant spec or CLAUDE.md

[[cipher_registry]]
== Cipher Registry

{voslc_cipher_s}
are declared as const values in `Tools/vok/vof/src/vofc_registry.rs`.
The VOF crate (Vox Obscura Foundation) is the single source of truth for all project prefixes across the entire VO/VV ecosystem.

=== Declaration Pattern

[source,rust]
----
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub struct vofc_Cipher {
    prefix: &'static str,
    project: &'static str,
}

impl vofc_Cipher {
    pub const fn new(prefix: &'static str, project: &'static str) -> Self {
        Self { prefix, project }
    }
}

// Example declarations
pub const JJ: vofc_Cipher = vofc_Cipher::new("jj", "Job Jockey");
pub const BU: vofc_Cipher = vofc_Cipher::new("bu", "Bash Utilities");
----

=== Uniqueness Guarantees

* **Compile-time**: duplicate const names are compilation errors
* **Runtime**: `vofc_validate_terminal_exclusivity()` verifies no prefix is a prefix of another
* **Convention**: new ciphers require coordinated addition to `vofc_registry.rs`

=== Complete Registry

See `Tools/vok/vof/src/vofc_registry.rs` for the authoritative list of all
{voslc_cipher_s}.
The registry is organized by domain:

* Voce Viva Ecosystem (VV, VO)
* Portable Kits (JJ, BU, CM, HM)
* Recipe Bottle Domain (RB, CRG)
* Tools and Utilities (GAD, CCC, LMCI, VSL)
* Concept Model Vocabulary (MCM, AXL)
* Other Projects (PB, WRS, SRF)

=== Utility Functions

[source,rust]
----
// Find cipher by prefix
pub fn vofc_find_by_prefix(prefix: &str) -> Option<&'static vofc_Cipher>;

// Check if prefix is registered
pub fn vofc_is_registered(prefix: &str) -> bool;

// Validate terminal exclusivity
pub fn vofc_validate_terminal_exclusivity() -> Option<(&'static vofc_Cipher, &'static vofc_Cipher)>;
----

content.text:
I've surveyed all the candidates plus the MCM grounding. Here's my informed read.

## What the survey turned up

**MCM (`mcm_sprue` / `mcm_inlay`)** — `mcm_sprue` is "a literal wire-level token... the exact character sequence as it appears in a wire format"; `mcm_inlay` is "a formal identifier following the prefix naming discipline... recognized by its prefix pattern." MCM itself states the load-bearing line: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also an inlay.* Critically, **no RBK/BUK spec currently cites these quoins** — they live only inside the CMK/AXLA constellation. So whatever host I pick, the MCM citation the paddock mandates is a *novel cross-constellation link*.

**VLS (Vox Liturgical Spec)** — the universal naming spec. Line 70: *"This vocabulary applies to ALL specifications — VOS, BUS, JJSA, and others."* It already defines **Ensign** = "a signet extension that classifies artifact category" with examples like `burc_regime` (`rc` classifies). The sprue pattern `<cipher><n><regime><e>_<value>` is *literally* a VLS construction: `rbnve` = `rb` cipher + `nve` ensign + `_value` epithet. Small (358 lines), focused, cross-project.

**BUS0** — already houses the prior `bubep_` exemplar (line 1144) and the `buv_enum_enroll` mechanism's vocabulary, voices an `axt_enum_value` motif, has a Regime Configuration spine. But it's **bash-dispatch-scoped** — the bulk of this heat is RBK regimes (RBRV/RBRN), which would be orphaned if their value-naming convention lived in a BUK spec.

**RB regime specs (RBSRV / RBRN)** — document *individual* values per variable (RBSRV: vessel/egress; RBRN: node modes + the excluded RBRN_RUNTIME), scattered. Right place for the per-variable value list, wrong place for a *cross-regime, cross-project convention*.

## My opinion: VLS, new section

The convention is fundamentally a **naming rule with cross-project reach** — it governs RB regimes *and* BUK's `BURN_PLATFORM`. Only VLS's stated scope matches that reach, and only VLS's existing vocabulary (Ensign) is the thing the prefix shape instantiates. The two vocabularies map cleanly onto the two layers of the convention:

- **What the values *are*** → `mcm_sprue` (wire-level tokens crossing rbrv.env / Cloud Build substitutions / JSON) — the MCM grounding the paddock insists on, justifying *why* these strings matter.
- **How the prefix is *built*** → VLS Signet + **Ensign** (`rbnve`/`rbnne` are category-classifying ensigns) — native VLS.
- **What the discipline *achieves*** → each sprue becomes an `mcm_inlay` (prefix-recognized).

A short cross-vocabulary bridge ("these values are `mcm_sprue`s; this section gives them VLS Ensign construction so they also become `mcm_inlay`s") is honest and load-bearing — and VLS→MCM is the most natural of the novel citations since both are naming-vocabulary specs.

**Runner-up: BUS0 Regime Configuration**, if you weight "the minter's hand is on `buv_enum_enroll`, which is BUK machinery" over "the minter is minting a name." The cost is orphaning RBK-regime discoverability.

content.text:
Fair — it surprised me a little too, in how cleanly it fits. Here's the orientation.

## VLS = Vox Liturgical Specification

`Tools/vok/vov_veiled/VLS-VoxLiturgicalSpec.adoc` (358 lines) — it's the **universal naming-system spec** that sits under VOK, parallel to MCM rather than inside it. Its own overview (line 70) claims the widest possible scope:

> *"This vocabulary applies to ALL specifications — VOS (kit distribution), BUS (bash dispatch), JJSA (gallops data), and others. The liturgy is the shared language for naming things."*

It formalizes the prefix-minting vocabulary that CLAUDE.md's "Prefix Naming Discipline" section gestures at in prose. The core terms:

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Cipher** (`voslc_`) | 2–5 char project root that seeds the namespace | `rb`, `bu`, `jj` |
| **Signet** (`vosls_`) | the complete prefix before the separator | `rbnve`, `burc` |
| **Ensign** (`vosln_`) | a signet extension that **classifies artifact category** | `burc_regime` — `rc` classifies as regime-config |
| **Monogram** (`voslm_`) | a signet extension binding to the epithet's initial | `buc_command` — `c` = command |
| **Epithet** (`vosle_`) | the descriptive word after the separator | `command`, `regime` |
| **Inscription** (`vosli_`) | the full name = signet + separator + epithet | `voslc_cipher` |
| **Vesture** (`voslv_`) | per-domain construction rules (Rust/Bash/AsciiDoc/git/slash) | snake_case, `_` sep, etc. |

The reason it leapt out at me: our sprue shape `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` *is* a VLS inscription. `rbnve_conjure` = `rb` cipher + `nve` **ensign** (the "n"=regime-namespace, "v"=vessel, "e"=enum-value classification) + `conjure` epithet. So the convention isn't a foreign thing we'd be bolting onto a naming spec — it's a specialization of a construction VLS already names (Ensign). That's what makes VLS a tighter fit than I'd have guessed before reading it.

The one wrinkle remains: VLS speaks the Vox vocabulary, not MCM, so the paddock-mandated `mcm_sprue`/`mcm_inlay` grounding would enter VLS as a cross-vocabulary bridge (the *what* — wire tokens) layered onto VLS's native *how* (Ensign construction). No spec currently cites MCM cross-constellation, so that's novel regardless of host — but VLS↔MCM is the most natural of those novel links since both are naming-authority specs.

Does that change your read? Same three options stand — VLS (my pick), BUS0 Regime Config, or an RB regime spec.

input.new_string:
[[bure_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{bure_prefix}::
Bash Utility Environment Regime

=== Regime Enum Sprue Convention

{axrg_regime_s} across BUK and the kits layered on it (Recipe Bottle's
RBRV/RBRN families among them) declare enumerated {axrg_variable_s} through
`buv_enum_enroll`.
This convention governs how the *values* of those enumerations are named, so
that each value is discoverable from its enum family by a single grep.

[[bus_enum_sprue]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value
{bus_enum_sprue}::
A regime enum value carried in prefix-disciplined form rather than as a bare
English word.
The value is a sprue in the MCM sense (`mcm_sprue`): a literal wire-level
token appearing verbatim in regime assignment files, Cloud Build
substitutions, build-info JSON, and shell/Python comparisons.
Bare, the token is invisible to its enum family; a recognizable prefix makes
it also an MCM inlay (`mcm_inlay`) — recognized by pattern, so a `grep` on
the family prefix returns the whole cluster.
+
Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>`
+
* `<proj>` — project cipher (`rb` Recipe Bottle, `bu` BUK)
* `<n>` — regime-namespace signal (`n`; `r` is taken by the regime-variable
  family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
* `<regime>` — regime family letter (`v` vessel, `n` node, `c` config,
  `s` station, `e` environment)
* `<e>` — enum-value signal
* `_<value>` — the canonical value name
+
Family-sharing is deliberate, not accidental: vessel-mode and egress-mode
both carry `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums share
`rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is voiced alike by ENTRY, DNS, and ACCESS.
Uniqueness lives in the full value plus its owning {axrg_variable}, not in
the shared prefix — one grep per family returns the complete cluster.

==== Target Enums

Vessel regime (`rbnve_`):

* `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE` — bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind` / `rbnve_conjure` / `rbnve_graft`
* `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE` — tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether` / `rbnve_airgap`

Node regime (`rbnne_`):

* `RBRN_ENTRY_MODE` — disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_enabled`
* `RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE` — disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_global` / `rbnne_allowlist`
* `RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE` — disabled/global/allowlist → same `rbnne_*` set

BURN node regime (`bunne_`):

* `BURN_PLATFORM` — linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux` / `bunne_mac` / `bunne_windows`

NOTE: `BURN_PLATFORM` is presently spelled `bubep_linux` / `bubep_mac` /
`bubep_windows` — an earlier, differently-shaped exemplar of the same idea.
The convention harmonizes it to `bunne_*` rather than grandfathering the
second shape.

==== Use-Site Classification

A bare-word grep on a value yields four classes; only two of them migrate.

. *Phase / concept references* — "the conjure phase", "the airgap build":
  name the workflow, not the enum value. *Leave bare.*
. *Enum-value references* — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals, and
  default-value defaults (`${z_mode:-conjure}`): the actual variable value.
  *Migrate.*
. *Identifier embeds* — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, sigils like
  `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like `ordain-conjure`: the
  word is part of a larger identifier. *Leave bare.*
. *Coupled derived strings* — the value drives a derived token living in
  resource-name space that must itself stay bare. *Leave the derived string
  bare; migrate only the dispatch that selects it.* Two confirmed witnesses:
  egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether` /
  `-airgap` fragments in pool paths), and vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes
  (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*`).
  Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace: the
  arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
  `-tether`.

==== RBRN_RUNTIME Exclusion

`RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker` / `podman`) is deliberately *excluded* — its value
stays bare.
It is consumed as the literal executable name
(`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`, `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`).
Adopting the convention would demand a permanent value→binary indirection
layer at every dispatch site — a runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability
of two values that `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks.
This is the one regime enum where the convention would cost more than it

{bure_prefix}::
Bash Utility Environment Regime

=== Regime Enum Sprue Convention

{axrg_regime_s} across BUK and the kits layered on it (Recipe Bottle's
RBRV/RBRN families among them) declare enumerated {axrg_variable_s} through
`buv_enum_enroll`.
This convention governs how the *values* of those enumerations are named, so
that each value is discoverable from its enum family by a single grep.

[[bus_enum_sprue]]
//axl_voices axt_enum_value
{bus_enum_sprue}::
A regime enum value carried in prefix-disciplined form rather than as a bare
English word.
The value is a sprue in the MCM sense (`mcm_sprue`): a literal wire-level
token appearing verbatim in regime assignment files, Cloud Build
substitutions, build-info JSON, and shell/Python comparisons.
Bare, the token is invisible to its enum family; a recognizable prefix makes
it also an MCM inlay (`mcm_inlay`) — recognized by pattern, so a `grep` on
the family prefix returns the whole cluster.
+
Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>`
+
* `<proj>` — project cipher (`rb` Recipe Bottle, `bu` BUK)
* `<n>` — regime-namespace signal (`n`; `r` is taken by the regime-variable
  family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
* `<regime>` — regime family letter (`v` vessel, `n` node, `c` config,
  `s` station, `e` environment)
* `<e>` — enum-value signal
* `_<value>` — the canonical value name
+
Family-sharing is deliberate, not accidental: vessel-mode and egress-mode
both carry `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums share
`rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is voiced alike by ENTRY, DNS, and ACCESS.
Uniqueness lives in the full value plus its owning {axrg_variable}, not in
the shared prefix — one grep per family returns the complete cluster.

==== Target Enums

Vessel regime (`rbnve_`):

* `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE` — bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind` / `rbnve_conjure` / `rbnve_graft`
* `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE` — tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether` / `rbnve_airgap`

Node regime (`rbnne_`):

* `RBRN_ENTRY_MODE` — disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_enabled`
* `RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE` — disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_global` / `rbnne_allowlist`
* `RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE` — disabled/global/allowlist → same `rbnne_*` set

BURN node regime (`bunne_`):

* `BURN_PLATFORM` — linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux` / `bunne_mac` / `bunne_windows`

NOTE: `BURN_PLATFORM` is presently spelled `bubep_linux` / `bubep_mac` /
`bubep_windows` — an earlier, differently-shaped exemplar of the same idea.
The convention harmonizes it to `bunne_*` rather than grandfathering the
second shape.

==== Use-Site Classification

A bare-word grep on a value yields four classes; only two of them migrate.

. *Phase / concept references* — "the conjure phase", "the airgap build":
  name the workflow, not the enum value. *Leave bare.*
. *Enum-value references* — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals, and
  default-value defaults (`${z_mode:-conjure}`): the actual variable value.
  *Migrate.*
. *Identifier embeds* — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, sigils like
  `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like `ordain-conjure`: the
  word is part of a larger identifier. *Leave bare.*
. *Coupled derived strings* — the value drives a derived token living in
  resource-name space that must itself stay bare. *Leave the derived string
  bare; migrate only the dispatch that selects it.* Two confirmed witnesses:
  egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether` /
  `-airgap` fragments in pool paths), and vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes
  (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*`).
  Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace: the
  arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
  `-tether`.

==== RBRN_RUNTIME Exclusion

`RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker` / `podman`) is deliberately *excluded* — its value
stays bare.
It is consumed as the literal executable name
(`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`, `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`).
Adopting the convention would demand a permanent value→binary indirection
layer at every dispatch site — a runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability
of two values that `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks.
This is the one regime enum where the convention would cost more than it
returns; the exclusion is a decision, not an oversight.

=== Kit Regime Operations
